on Feb. 19: get tiltmeter data at Longmire. Meeting with William For one split on one window, how is that single window determined? How is it done for Christian's version, for MFAST, and for SWSPy? Selecting a single window MFAST: Performs hierarchical clustering over many windows. For one window, MFAST starts by picking a dataset-dependent initial window for first calculating SNR. Then the frequency analysis window is defined as 1.5 × the initial window. Then there is a spectral estimate over the frequency analysis window, computes a power spectrum, and picks the dominant frequency as the frequency of maximum spectral power. There are a couple of constraints on the dominant frequency to make sure it is at least 1 / snr\_window\_period and at most fs / 10. So now we have a dominant frequency, then we calculate the dominant period as Tmid = 1 / fd. Then Tmid is used to construct a series of windows, with the shortest equal to the length of Tmid and the longest equal to 2.5 × Tmid. The number of window start and end times is set by the user. All of the windows, of varying lengths, can be calculated from this information. So the dominant period is used to set up the windows. SWSPy: Performs density-based clustering over many windows. Baillard: Looks at one single window. Defines one S-wave window based on the data, then computes spectral density to find dominant frequency in the initial window. Sets the window as 3 × T\_dom, so it gets multiple cycles and can produce a meaningful result. So it's quite different from MFAST in that the dominant period is directly used as the analysis window, where the dominant period is used to set up the lengths of windows that will be considered in MFAST. It's difficult to compare SWSPy vs. Baillard 1:1 by plots because the SWSPy splitting is done in LQT space rather than ZNE, but the output direction is φ degrees from North. It's possible that Baillard and SWSPy are defining fast direction by different conventions — I will check. Here's the discrepancy: Baillard defines fast direction by trigonometric convention, as CCW from East. SWSPy defines fast direction as degrees CW from North. Conversion factor (in degrees): φ\_{CCW from E} = − (φ\_{CW from N} + 90), then convert to radians with × π / 180. We find that there is still some discrepancy between SWSPy and Baillard after conversion, but it's relatively small. Same with the discrepancy in delay time. This makes sense, as the global minima are often a region rather than one particular spot. MFAST uses the convention of CW from North as well, rather than CCW from East. This is consistent with Silver and Chan (1991), and the azimuth convention in seismology. Thus, we see that SWSPy returns results relatively consistent with Baillard's method, and it already integrates a method of windowing and clustering. Since this is a published methodology that already integrates clustering and is fully built in Python, we should just use SWSPy for the shear-wave splitting. February 12, 2026 Part of \[\[Axial-Splitting]]. I met with William this morning and discussed how one window is calculated between MFAST, Baillard, and SWSPy. He gave some good feedback on next steps to get to the point where I can run my analysis on lots of data using Kaiwen's new catalog. Steps 1\. Read MFAST, SWSPy original papers and documentation. How are start and end windows determined? MFAST: In their example study, at a volcano, they suggest 2–5 start times and ≥10 end times. MFAST builds on Teanby by grading clusters on multiple characteristics, rather than just minimal variance, and using the frequency of the seismogram to determine the lengths of analysis windows. They use 5 start times, Nwbeg. They used time-step size between window start times dTbeg = 0.2 at start, decreased to the smaller of 0.1 s or ((S-P)/2) / Nwbeg. They use time-step size between window end times dTend to start at 0.08 s, increasing or decreasing to make Nwend between 15 and 25. The number of end times tested, Nwend = int((w4 - w3) / dTend). The desired last window end time, w4 = Stime + Tmid × 2.5 + 0.15. So it's 2.5 cycles of the dominant frequency plus a little 0.15 s buffer at the end. Tend0 = w3 is the earliest window end time, defined as Stime + Tmid/1.2 + 0.15. So it's ⅚ of the first cycle plus a little buffer. This makes sense — the number of end times is proportional to the total range of end times divided by the step size. The number of start times is just set. Why? They pick 5 start times that will essentially go from just before the S-pick time to just after, scaling by the S-P time to avoid P-wave contamination. The first time to start a window, Tbeg0, is defined as 0.3 seconds before the S-pick, but decreased to the smaller of 0.1 or ((S-P)/2) / (Nbeg + 1). The last time to start a window is Tbeg1 = S\_time − (Tbeg0 + (Nbeg × dTbeg)). So the window start times go from 0.1 seconds before the S-pick or a fraction of the S-P time before the S-pick, to that value plus the number of beginning start

time to start a window, Tbeg0, is defined as 0.3 seconds before the S-pick, but decreased to the smaller of 0.1 or ((S-P)/2) / (Nbeg + 1). The last time to start a window is Tbeg1 = S\_time − (Tbeg0 + (Nbeg × dTbeg)). So the window start times go from 0.1 seconds before the S-pick or a fraction of the S-P time before the S-pick, to that value plus the number of beginning start times times the number of step sizes for the start windows. Again, dTbeg is calculated as 0.1 or a fraction of the S-P time. SWSPy: The user specifies the S-wave arrival time uncertainty, called win\_S\_pick\_tolerance, the earliest possible start time of any window, called overall\_win\_start\_pre\_fast\_S\_pick, and the earliest possible end time of any window, called overall\_win\_start\_post\_fast\_S\_pick. These are all defined relative to the S-pick time, so the uncertainty is quite important. They note that there are methods of automatically calculating the windowing parameters using spectral estimates, but that is not currently implemented in SWSPy, and they leave it to future contributors who deem it important enough to implement. It should be noted that win\_S\_pick\_tolerance is one value, but it also designates the time before and after the S-pick to not allow windows to start within, in seconds. So this is a ± buffer around the S-pick that shifts the window start times. max\_t\_shift\_s is the maximum time to shift the data by, in seconds. n\_win is the number of start and end times to pick, which is currently constant window step sizes within the specified range. They compare their results to similar packages, including MFAST, and find identical splitting results within uncertainty. What's not immediately obvious is whether the window lengths actually vary. What is the time-step size? Is it the same time-step size between the possible start times and possible end times? Based on Fig. 4 in the main shear-wave splitting paper, it looks like you have S-pick minus S-pick tolerance, and you have the first pre-arrival window time, overall\_win\_start\_pre\_fast\_S\_pick, and you define a number of windows. Then all the window start times are each window in the range (S-pick tolerance − overall\_win\_start\_pre\_fast\_S\_pick) / n\_win. Which, in their example, is (0.2 s before S-pick − 0.1 s before S-pick)

the possible start times and possible end times? Based on Fig. 4 in the main shear-wave splitting paper, it looks like you have S-pick minus S-pick tolerance, and you have the first pre-arrival window time, overall\_win\_start\_pre\_fast\_S\_pick, and you define a number of windows. Then all the window start times are each window in the range (S-pick tolerance − overall\_win\_start\_pre\_fast\_S\_pick) / n\_win. Which, in their example, is (0.2 s before S-pick − 0.1 s before S-pick) / 10. So that length is 0.1 seconds, and there is a window start time every 0.01 seconds. Then the end windows have the same number of windows and same effective step sizes as the starting windows, with the first window end time at overall\_win\_start\_post\_fast\_S\_pick. In their example, that's 0.3 seconds after the S-pick. The end times then end 0.1 seconds afterwards, with 10 possible end times in that period. So now you have 10 window start times and 10 window end times, and each combination of windows is a window for analysis. The longest window, in their example, goes from 0.2 s before the S-pick to 0.4 s after the S-pick. The shortest window goes from 0.1 s before the S-pick to 0.3 s after the S-pick. 2\. Project Takeover — September 2025 Part of \[\[Axial-Splitting]]. As of September 2025, I, Michael Hemmett, have taken over the Axial Shear-Wave Splitting project working with William Wilcock and Marine Denolle. The project was started by Christian Baillard in 2018–19, but it had not been worked on since then. Christian's original project used the Axial Seamount catalog with original locations for all permanent stations, from January 22, 2015 through the summer of 2017. He used MFAST with SAC and TauP to perform the analysis, based on Silver and Chan (1991) for the original splitting, and with the windowing and clustering method of Teanby et al. (2004). All of his plotting and analysis was done in Python, and I have access to most scripts and results in the Axial Splitting Google Drive. Christian had results for this whole time period; he'd presented a poster on the results and had a manuscript in progress, as well as a literature review up to date through 2019. I sought to replicate Christian's results exactly while using the most up-to-date tools for maximum reproducibility: everything should be done in Python, not also Fortran, C++, etc. First, I developed a shear-wave splitting workflow from data loading, to pre-processing, to splitting, all done in Python. I then began comparing my results with Christian's results so as to exactly replicate what he did. Christian spent a long time identifying which parameters would be most suitable for this specific application of SWS at Axial using S-phases, so we want to retain all that work he did by exactly replicating his results. I opted to use the SWSPy package developed by Tom Hudson as a suitable alternative to MFAST. Comparing the MFAST manual and Christian's implementation of it, I had to locally adapt the package to exactly replicate the method in MFAST, following Silver and Chan and Teanby. Axial Splitting — Project Hub Central note for the shear-wave splitting (SWS) project at Axial Seamount. Source code lives in ~/Seismology/axial-splitting-ml. This note tracks scope, methods, code structure, current status, and links out to topic notes and daily logs. Scientific Goal Track the temporal evolution of stress and crack-induced anisotropy in Axial Seamount's caldera through shear-wave splitting on local seismicity. The long-term arc starts just before the April 2015 eruption and runs through to the present, currently re-inflated state of stress. Splitting parameters — fast polarization direction (φ) and delay time (δt) — encode the orientation and density of aligned cracks in the shear-wave window. Tracking how these change in space and time should constrain how the volcanic stress field reorganizes around eruptive episodes and feed into forecasting work on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. Key questions: Does φ rotate before/during/after the 2015 eruption? Do δt magnitudes track the inflation cycle (more cracks open → larger δt)? Are the anisotropy patterns spatially coherent across the caldera or station-dependent? How sensitive are these signals to methodological choices (windowing, clustering, QC)? Data Network: OOI Regional Cabled Array (RCA), University of Washington Period: 2015–2021 (current); long-term goal is pre-2015 → present Instrumentation: short-period and broadband ocean-bottom seismometers (OBS) Stations Five caldera-floor stations carry the eruption-window analysis: AXAS1, AXAS2 — south caldera flank AXEC1, AXEC2, AXEC3 — eastern caldera floor AXCC1 (central caldera) tipped over in March 2015 due to caldera volumetric inflation; it'll be folded back in once orientation/tilt corrections are settled. AXID1 sits on the cabled array but isn't part of the eruption-window five. Future additions: the 15-station 2022–2024 temporary OBS deployment along the North Rift Zone. Catalogs The production catalog is the MLdd (machine-learning + double-difference) catalog from Kaiwen Wang's work at Axial Seamount (data/Axial.MLDD.v202112.2, parsed into data/mldd\_catalog\_2015\_2021.csv). S-pick uncertainties from Wang (2024) drive the per-event windowing. Method development and ongoing cross-validation use a NonLinLoc 3D relocated catalog with kurtosis-based picks from Wilcock & Baillard (data/AXIAL.PHASE.FINAL\_3D\_V2.nlloc). Results between the two pipelines are consistent — the NonLinLoc catalog now serves mostly as a regression check. | Catalog | Role | File | |---|---|---| | Wang MLdd, 2015–2021 | primary | data/Axial.MLDD.v202112.2 → mldd\_catalog\_2015\_2021.csv | | Wilcock/Baillard NonLinLoc | method dev + cross-validation | data/AXIAL.PHASE.FINAL\_3D\_V2.nlloc | Station metadata

Method Production analysis uses a modified SWSPy (Silver & Chan 1991 eigenvalue minimization with Teanby et al. 2004 cluster analysis) with several MFAST-style adjustments and per-event dynamic parameters. Christian Baillard's original single-window Python implementation is the historical foundation of this work — many of his supporting scripts are still active dependencies — and provides the cross-validation reference. Modified SWSPy (production) Vendored in swspy/ with the following corrections relative to upstream: Hierarchical Ward clustering (replacing upstream DBSCAN) in a circular-safe (δt cos 2φ, δt sin 2φ) coordinate space — matches MFAST. Teanby (2004) representative variance (Eq. 13–14) for cluster selection; best observation within cluster is the one with minimum observation variance. Adapted windowing and clustering parameters relative to upstream. Dynamic windowing on T\_dom of each event (similar to MFAST), rather than fixed window lengths. Wang (2024) S-pick uncertainty drives the analysis-window placement around the S arrival. Core: swspy/swspy/splitting/split.py::\_sws\_win\_clustering, plus the splitting\_functions.py workflow in scripts/. Christian Baillard's single-window method (foundation + validation) Much of the early method work, and a large fraction of the supporting scripts in this repo, draws on Baillard's unpublished Python implementation: Single adaptive window, sized to \[0.02, T\_dom × 2.0]. 100 lags × 100 angles exhaustive grid search per event. Multiple-minima detection on the eigenvalue surface (morphological filtering). RMS-based ranking of solutions; P-coda contamination handling. His scripts (shearwavesplit.py, sws\_methods.py, plotwaveform.py, GMT.py, projection.py, util.py, sws\_vertices.py) are still active dependencies — splitting\_functions.py imports them transitively. Cross-validation against his reference results: SNR within ~5%, geometry within 1°, φ/δt consistent. noteTitle: Joint Inversion - Unified Stress Theory Framework
text: A Unified Theoretical Framework for Joint Stress Inversion from Focal Mechanisms, Shear-Wave Splitting, and dv/v Working Document April 2026 1\. Introduction and Motivation This document develops the theoretical chain connecting three seismological observables — P-wave focal mechanisms, shear-wave splitting (SWS), and relative seismic velocity variations (dv/v) — to the in-situ stress tensor at Axial Seamount. Each observable senses a different projection of the stress field through distinct physical mechanisms, but all three ultimately depend on the same underlying quantities: the stress tensor σ\_ij, the crack/damage state of the medium, and the resulting elastic stiffness tensor C\_ijkl. The goal is to formalize a single forward model: σ\_ij(x, t) → crack state → C\_ijkl(x, t) → {focal mechanisms, SWS parameters, dv/v} such that a joint inversion of all three data types yields a better-constrained stress tensor than any individual observable alone. The central physical insight is this: differential stress controls the distribution and state of microcracks (and macroscopic faults), which in turn control the fourth-order elastic stiffness tensor C\_ijkl. That tensor governs wave propagation (hence splitting and velocities) and, through the Wallace-Bott hypothesis, fault slip directions (hence focal mechanisms). The stress tensor is the common cause; C\_ijkl is the common mediator. 2\. The Stress Tensor and Its Decomposition The Cauchy stress tensor σ\_ij is a symmetric second-order tensor with six independent components. At any point in the medium it can be decomposed as: where σ\_kk = σ\_11 + σ\_22 + σ\_33 is the trace (three times the mean stress), δ\_ij is the Kronecker delta, and s\_ij = σ\_ij − (1/3)σ\_kk δ\_ij is the deviatoric stress tensor (traceless, five independent components). In principal coordinates, σ\_ij is diagonal with eigenvalues σ\_1 ≥ σ\_2 ≥ σ\_3 (principal stresses, compression positive). The stress ratio: parameterizes the shape of the stress ellipsoid. R = 0 means σ\_1 = σ\_2 (oblate, uniaxial compression), R = 1 means σ\_2 = σ\_3 (prolate, uniaxial extension), and R = 0.5 is triaxial. Why this decomposition matters for the joint inversion: Focal mechanisms constrain the deviatoric stress tensor (four parameters: three Euler angles for principal axes + R), but NOT the isotropic/mean stress (Michael, 1984). dv/v is sensitive to both isotropic (mean stress / pore pressure) and deviatoric components through the acoustoelastic effect and crack closure. text: = σ\_2 (oblate, uniaxial compression), R = 1 means σ\_2 = σ\_3 (prolate, uniaxial extension), and R = 0.5 is triaxial. Why this decomposition matters for the joint inversion: Focal mechanisms constrain the deviatoric stress tensor (four parameters: three Euler angles for principal axes + R), but NOT the isotropic/mean stress (Michael, 1984). dv/v is sensitive to both isotropic (mean stress / pore pressure) and deviatoric components through the acoustoelastic effect and crack closure. SWS is sensitive primarily to the deviatoric stress through crack alignment, but crack density is also affected by mean effective stress. This complementarity is the fundamental reason a joint inversion is more powerful than any individual method. 3\. Observable 1: P-Wave Focal Mechanisms → Stress Tensor 3.1 The Wallace-Bott Hypothesis The foundational assumption linking earthquake slip to stress was articulated by Wallace (1951) and Bott (1959): slip on a pre-existing fault plane occurs in the direction of maximum resolved shear stress on that plane. For a fault with unit normal n and slip direction d, the traction vector on the fault plane is: The resolved shear traction in the slip direction is: The Wallace-Bott hypothesis states that d is parallel to the shear traction component of T projected onto the fault plane: That is, the slip direction equals the direction of maximum resolved shear stress on the plane, which is the projection of the traction vector onto the fault surface minus the normal component. 3.2 Formal Stress Inversion from Focal Mechanisms (Michael, 1984, 1987) Michael (1984) formalized the inverse problem: given N focal mechanisms (each specifying a fault normal n^(k) and slip vector d^(k)), find the stress tensor σ\_ij that best satisfies the Wallace-Bott hypothesis for all events. The key relationship for each event k is: where |τ^(k)| is the magnitude of the resolved shear stress (a normalization factor). Since |τ^(k)| differs for each event, the absolute stress magnitude cancels. This means: Focal mechanism inversion constrains only the orientations of the principal stress axes (σ\_1, σ\_2, σ\_3) and the stress ratio R. It does NOT constrain the absolute magnitudes of the principal stresses or the mean stress. In practice, the inversion solves for a reduced stress tensor (normalized so that the maximum differential stress = 1). The full solution space is

It does NOT constrain the absolute magnitudes of the principal stresses or the mean stress. In practice, the inversion solves for a reduced stress tensor (normalized so that the maximum differential stress = 1). Assumptions in the Michael (1984) method: Stress is uniform across the fault population (within each inversion volume/time window). Earthquakes occur on pre-existing faults of diverse orientations (not all faults are optimally oriented). Fault slip directions are independent (one fault's slip does not influence another's). The fault plane (vs. auxiliary plane) ambiguity must be resolved or marginalized. 3.3 Angelier's Method and Extensions Angelier (1979, 1984) developed complementary methods for stress tensor determination from fault-slip data (originally for geological field data). The mathematical framework is equivalent — minimize the angular misfit between observed slip directions and the predicted direction of maximum resolved shear stress — but the implementation differs. Angelier's approach uses direct grid search over (σ\_1, σ\_2, σ\_3 orientations, R) space. 3.4 Bayesian and Damped Approaches Arnold and Townend (2007) introduced a Bayesian framework (implemented in the SATSI algorithm) that accounts for uncertainties in nodal plane orientations and provides posterior probability distributions on the stress parameters. This is important for quantifying uncertainty when combining with other observables. Hardebeck and Michael (2006) developed a damped regional-scale stress inversion (DRSSI) that allows spatial variation in the stress field by solving simultaneously for stress on a grid, with a damping constraint that penalizes rapid spatial variation. This is directly relevant for mapping stress across the Axial Seamount caldera. 3.5 Temporal Stress Inversion Martínez-Garzón et al. (2014, 2016) applied time-dependent stress inversion at volcanic and geothermal systems, showing that temporal windowing of focal mechanism catalogs can track the evolution of stress orientation and R through eruption cycles and injection episodes. The key is having sufficient focal mechanisms per time window (typically ≥ 15–20) for a stable inversion. Massa et al. Stress orientation ambiguity in SWS: In regions with multiple crack sets (common near volcanoes with complex structure), the fast direction φ may not simply reflect σ\_H. Focal mechanisms provide an independent constraint on stress orientation that disambiguates the SWS interpretation. Absolute stress scale: Neither focal mechanisms nor SWS constrain absolute stress. But dv/v, combined with laboratory-calibrated velocity-stress relationships and a known reference state, can provide an estimate of absolute stress changes, anchoring the relative measurements from the other two methods. Non-uniqueness in C\_ijkl: The inverse problem of going from SWS observations to C\_ijkl is non-unique (many stiffness tensors can produce the same splitting for a given ray geometry). Adding dv/v constrains the isotropic part of C\_ijkl. Adding focal mechanisms constrains the stress tensor that must be consistent with that C\_ijkl through the crack model. Together, the non-uniqueness is substantially reduced. 8\. Formulation of the Joint Inverse Problem 8.1 Model Parameters The model parameters to be determined at each point x and time t are: Primary unknowns (what we solve for): σ\_ij(x, t): 6 independent stress components (or equivalently σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ orientations + magnitudes) P\_p(x, t): pore pressure Auxiliary/structural parameters (determined separately or jointly): v\_P⁰(x), v\_S⁰(x): reference (crack-free or reference-state) velocity model N₀(α, θ, φ; x): initial crack population distribution Rheological parameters: friction coefficient μ\_f, Biot coefficient η 8.2 Forward Model (Summary) 8.3 Misfit Function The joint inversion minimizes a combined misfit: where: Focal mechanism misfit: (sum over events k of the angular misfit between observed and predicted slip directions) SWS misfit: (sum over SWS measurements of squared residuals in splitting intensity) dv/v misfit: (sum over station pairs (i,j) and time windows t) Regularization: (spatial and temporal smoothing of

This can be done by normalizing each misfit by the number of data and the expected variance, or by L-curve analysis. 8.4 Inversion Strategy The forward model contains nonlinear relationships (especially the stress → crack state step), so the inversion will likely require iterative linearization: Initialize with the focal mechanism stress inversion result (Michael, 1984) for stress orientations and R. Use dv/v as a proxy for initial mean stress variations. Forward compute C\_ijkl from the initial stress model using the crack model. Compute predicted observables for all three data types. Compute Fréchet derivatives (sensitivity of each observable to perturbations in σ\_ij and P\_p). These can be computed analytically for the crack model or numerically by finite differencing. Update the model by solving the linearized inverse problem (e.g., using damped least squares or conjugate gradient methods). Iterate until convergence. The linearization at each iteration uses the Jacobian matrix: which has block structure reflecting the three data types. 9\. Resolving Non-uniqueness in C\_ijkl 9.1 The Problem The elastic stiffness tensor C\_ijkl has 21 independent components (for the most general triclinic symmetry). Even with the assumption of crack-induced anisotropy (which reduces the degrees of freedom through the crack model), inferring C\_ijkl from limited seismic observations is highly non-unique. 9.2 How Each Observable Constrains C\_ijkl SWS constrains the anisotropic part of C\_ijkl (differences between stiffness in different directions), particularly the components that control S-wave velocity differences. dv/v constrains the isotropic average of C\_ijkl (or more precisely, changes in the directionally averaged moduli). Focal mechanisms constrain the stress tensor, which, through the crack model, constrains the physical mechanism generating the anisotropy in C\_ijkl. 9.3 The Role of the

The crack model then predicts the full C\_ijkl tensor self-consistently. This reduces the effective number of unknowns from 21 (C\_ijkl) to ~7 per point (6 stress components + 1 pore pressure), plus global parameters describing the initial crack population. The physics ensures that the resulting C\_ijkl is always consistent with a physically realizable crack distribution. 10\. Specific Considerations for Axial Seamount 10.1 Geological Context Axial Seamount is a mid-ocean ridge hotspot volcano on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. Its stress field is controlled by: Regional plate-tectonic stress: Ridge-perpendicular extension (σ₃ ≈ E-W at this latitude). Magmatic pressure: Inflation/deflation of the shallow magma reservoir beneath the caldera (Nooner and Chadwick, 2016). Caldera ring faults: Localized stress concentrations along the caldera boundary (Levy et al., various; Hefner et al., 2020). Hydrothermal circulation: Pore pressure variations driven by fluid flow. The eruption cycle (Nooner and Chadwick, 2016) involves progressive inflation (increasing magma pressure → increasing σ\_H → changing anisotropy and dv/v) punctuated by eruptions (rapid deflation → stress reversal). 10.2 Expected Signatures in Each Observable Pre-eruption inflation: Focal mechanisms: possible rotation of σ₁ toward vertical near the magma chamber (increasing contribution of magma pressure to vertical stress); R may change. SWS: φ may rotate to reflect the evolving stress pattern around the inflating source; δt should increase as differential stress increases and cracks align. dv/v: should decrease (increasing pore pressure and/or crack opening from magma-induced stress). Michael Hemmett Constraining Stress Evolution Across an Eruption Cycle at Axial Seamount Volcano Plate tectonics drive many of the processes responsible for significant geohazards, from arc volcanism to massive subduction zone earthquakes (USGS, 2025). One key part of this cycle is seafloor spreading, where mantle magma upwells and erupts to form new oceanic crust at mid-ocean ridge sites where plates diverge. This creation of new oceanic crust – from mantle upwelling through magma storage and eruption – occurs through the eruption cycle of a mid-ocean ridge volcano (Tolstoy et al., 2006). One useful lens for understanding the dynamics of seafloor spreading includes constraining the geophysical stress field of a volcano and its evolution across an eruption cycle. It is critically important to constrain this process, but the complex geology and dynamics of a volcano, limitations in offshore instrumentation, and the issue of many non-linear, anisotropic components of the stress-strain relationship have thus far limited progress in this area of research. I will perform a joint stress inversion with three distinct studies on geophysical stress – focal mechanisms, shear-wave splitting, and relative seismic velocity variations – to computationally model and quantitatively constrain stress evolution across the Axial Seamount caldera over an entire eruption cycle. Axial Seamount, the best instrumented submarine volcano in the world (Wilcock et al., 2018), is well-poised to constrain these critical processes. Many studies have probed the geology and structure of Axial Seamount, such that its dominant features controlling stress evolution can be accurately modeled (Chadwick and Nooner, 2016; Hefner et al., 2020; Slead et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2026; Kidiwela et al., in prep). Ongoing work at Axial Seamount is already constraining the geophysical stress field from these three different perspectives, though individually (Zhang et al., in prep; Wang et al., in prep). All three stress studies are built on a high quality machine learning-based earthquake catalog relocated with the double difference algorithm (Wang et al., 2024), lending further confidence to these measurements and the ability to interpret them jointly. P-wave focal mechanisms constrain stress orientation and relative contributions of components to the stress tensor (Massa et al., 2016). studies are built on a high quality machine learning-based earthquake catalog relocated with the double difference algorithm (Wang et al., 2024), lending further confidence to these measurements and the ability to interpret them jointly. P-wave focal mechanisms constrain stress orientation and relative contributions of components to the stress tensor (Massa et al., 2016). Shear-wave splitting parameters constrain the orientation of maximum horizontal compressional stress and changes in percent anisotropy across time (Silver and Chan, 1991; Kendall et al., 2025). This can be combined with a high quality S-wave velocity model, ray tracing and the splitting intensity measure (Chevrot, 2000) to model anisotropy in 4D. Ambient noise interferometry reveals relative changes in seismic wave propagation velocities (dv/v), which are related to anisotropy, pore pressure, and changes in the stress field (Donaldson et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2024). The primary challenge presented is the need for a unified theory of geophysical stress that integrates all three perspectives. This study will develop a model for stress evolution at Axial Seamount volcano, integrated with a real-time monitoring framework for eruption prediction, while also advancing the fundamental understanding of geophysical stress and the seafloor spreading cycle. This framework for joint stress inversion will serve as an example for future monitoring on Cascade arc volcanoes that threaten major population centers. References Chevrot, S. (2000). J. Geophys.
